channel.title: Crypto Research Notes
channel.description: Original long-form research on crypto research methodology, on-chain data analysis, tokenomics, smart contract security, DAO governance, oracles, cross-chain bridge security, and Sybil address detection. For learning and research only — not investment advice.

item.title: On-Chain AI Agent Verification: When Automation Holds Your Keys, Who Sets the Limits
item.description: The opener of the "AI × On-Chain" series. As more products claim an AI agent trades, compounds and rebalances for you, one downplayed fact is that to spend your money on-chain it must hold signing authority over your funds. This article is not about how smart the model is, but a more fundamental question — who sets the permission boundary and what enforces it. It covers key- and signing-custody models, on-chain hard caps on per-transaction and cumulative spending, traceability of every action, the new attack surface of prompt injection and external data dependence, and a reusable stress-test checklist. Mechanism principles only; names no real product; not investment advice.

item.title: Prediction Market Resolution Risk: Who Actually Holds Final Authority
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into prediction market resolution mechanisms: why real-world event adjudication is a fundamentally different verification problem from price oracles; how the propose-and-dispute mechanism actually works and its key parameters; whether final adjudication authority sits with a token vote, a committee, or a dedicated arbitration protocol, and whether adjudicators overlap with market participants in ways that create manipulation incentives; whether the evidence behind a resolution is traceable and whether the protocol has pre-specified rules for ambiguous events; and how to stress-test the manipulation and delayed-settlement cost of a high-value, ambiguous-outcome market. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Credit Pool Tranche Risk: What Does a Senior Share Actually Get You
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into real-world credit pools: why these pools differ fundamentally from overcollateralized DeFi lending, since the real security is a borrower's off-chain creditworthiness and legal agreement rather than on-chain collateral; how senior/junior tranche structure determines loss order, and whether junior sizing matches real default-rate data; how to verify whether disclosed APY and historical default rates are backed by independent third parties and cover a credit-tightening cycle; whether borrower due diligence and concentration are independently verifiable; and how to stress-test the loss distribution across tranches during a credit-tightening cycle. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Decentralized Options Vaults: The Stable Weekly Yield Is Actually a Tail-Risk Premium
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into decentralized options vaults (DOVs): depositor assets are used to write options and what's collected is a premium, not interest, distinguishing covered-call and cash-secured-put structures; why a stable weekly yield corresponds to a real directional risk exposure, since an assignment event is a structural norm rather than an anomaly; how to verify whether disclosed APY nets in real settlement losses from assignment rather than only showing premium income; whether strike selection and delta management are verifiable and whether the protocol publishes real-time position parameters; and how to stress-test how much vault NAV could drop during a single settlement period. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Perpetual DEX Vaults: LPs Aren't Bystanders — They're the Counterparty to Every Trade
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into vault-model perpetual DEXs: why there is no separate insurance fund and LP depositors are directly the counterparty to every trader's position; how depositor risk actually works, with vault net asset value dropping directly when traders are collectively profitable; how to verify whether a disclosed APY nets in payouts to profitable traders or only shows fee income; whether a protocol's claimed skew-management and hedging mechanisms actually exist and are enforced; and how to stress-test how much vault NAV could drop during a strong sustained trend. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Vote-Escrow Token Locks: Does the Claimed Lock Duration Actually Remove Supply from Circulation
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into vote-escrow (ve) token locking mechanisms: why a claim that tokens are locked requires verification rather than being an automatic fact; how to verify on-chain contract lock duration and unlock progress against marketing claims; how liquid wrapper tokens let locked positions be re-tokenized and effectively exited early, undermining the meaning of the lock; why acquiring voting power through locking does not necessarily mean genuine long-term economic alignment; and how to compare disclosed locked supply against on-chain contract balances while mapping the unlock-expiry distribution. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real protocol or token, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Auto-Deleveraging: When the Insurance Fund Runs Dry, Whose Position Gets Closed
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into perpetual futures auto-deleveraging (ADL): why it exists as the last line of defense in the liquidation waterfall once the insurance fund is exhausted; how ADL ranks counterparties by profit percentage and leverage, and why profitable positions get force-closed first rather than randomly; how to verify whether an exchange's claimed insurance fund size is actually checkable; why the absence of historical ADL trigger disclosure is itself a risk signal; and how to stress-test a single-symbol liquidation cascade that exhausts the insurance fund. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real exchange or protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Liquid Staking Tokens: Why the Derivative Token's Price Isn't Always Equal to the Underlying Staked Asset
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into the trust structure behind liquid staking tokens: why the token is a claim on a validator asset pool rather than an instantly redeemable asset; how the speed mismatch between instant secondary-market liquidity and the native protocol redemption queue creates price deviation from 1:1; whether slashing losses are shared proportionally across all holders; how to verify validator and operator concentration; and what instant secondary-market tradability conceals under mass-redemption stress. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real liquid staking protocol or token, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: RWA Tokenization: The On-Chain Token Is Just a Pointer to an Off-Chain Asset
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into the trust structure behind RWA tokenization: why an on-chain token is a pointer to an off-chain asset rather than the asset itself; what legal right a holder actually has to the underlying asset, and why enforceable redemption rights and bankruptcy remoteness are the variables that matter; how the same verification method our proof-of-reserves article described for exchanges applies to RWA custodians and disclosure practices; whether on-chain mint and burn records actually track off-chain asset scale over time; and how 24/7 on-chain tradability can mask a liquidity mismatch between the token and the slower-moving underlying asset, creating run risk under stress. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real RWA protocol, issuer, or custodian, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: MEV and Private Order Flow: The Sandwich Attack Didn't Disappear, It Just Changed Hands
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into MEV and private order flow: why MEV is the price of ordering power rather than a hidden tax, the full three-transaction structure of a sandwich attack and its direct causal link to slippage tolerance, how private transaction channels relocate MEV-extraction capability from public participants to a small number of block builders, why builder concentration under proposer-builder separation (PBS) is the structural risk metric that matters, and how a researcher should shift from asking whether MEV exists to asking where it goes. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real exchange, builder, or wallet product, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Funding Rate Divergence: The Most Misread Signal in Perpetual Futures
item.description: This article takes a deep dive into the perpetual futures funding-rate mechanism: why funding rate is the core mechanism anchoring contract price to spot rather than a simple holding cost, how to decompose the rate into its interest and premium components, why mark price can still diverge from index price, the real risk composition of funding-rate arbitrage strategies, and how open interest and liquidation-price clustering together signal a coming liquidation cascade. The entire piece discusses abstract mechanism principles only — it does not name any real exchange or contract product, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying Stablecoin Pegs: Does the Redemption Path Actually Deliver on the $1 Promise
item.description: This article introduces research methods for stablecoin peg mechanisms: how the three collateral models (over-collateralized, algorithmic, and reserve-backed) each fail differently, how to stress-test whether the mint/redeem arbitrage path actually holds up under pressure, how to verify correlated-collapse risk between a stablecoin's collateral and its depeg triggers, and how to independently research a stablecoin's actual depeg history and recovery mechanisms. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real stablecoin, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying On-Chain Insurance: Coverage Looks Broad Until You File a Claim
item.description: This article introduces research methods for on-chain insurance/cover protocols: how to verify a policy's actual coverage triggers in the terms rather than marketing language, how to assess a cover pool's claims-paying capacity and correlated-collapse risk, how to decompose underwriter yield into real premium income versus token subsidies, and how governance concentration shapes claims-arbitration outcomes. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying Restaking: How Slashing Risk Stacks When One Collateral Backs Multiple Protocols
item.description: This article introduces research methods for restaking mechanisms: how restaking yield is composed of base staking yield plus stacked AVS reward layers, why stacked yield also means stacked independent slashing risk, how to verify a liquid restaking token's (LRT) discount to its underlying redemption value, and how operator concentration creates a cross-AVS attack surface. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real protocol, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying Swap Routing: What Costs Hide Behind the “Best Path” Quote
item.description: This article introduces research methods for cross-chain swap aggregation and routing: how an aggregator computes a best path between single-hop and multi-hop routes, how to decompose a quote into slippage, bridge fees, and destination-chain gas reserves, why more hops don't automatically mean a better net result, and how to verify a routing contract's own permissions and bridge-selection transparency. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real aggregator or bridge, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Vote-Bribery Markets: When Governance Votes Have a Price Tag, Is It Still Governance
item.description: This article introduces research methods for governance vote-locking and bribe markets: how lock mechanisms translate into tradable voting power, why bribe-market economics work, how to calculate bribe ROI and distinguish genuine governance participants from mercenary vote-sellers, and the hidden centralization risk introduced by vote-aggregator platforms. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real project, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Liquidity Mining Yield: How Much Does Impermanent Loss Eat Into That APY
item.description: This article introduces research methods for liquidity mining: how to decompose a blended APY into real fee revenue and token-emission rewards, how to independently calculate impermanent loss and compare it against reward income, how to spot mercenary capital and farm-and-dump patterns, and how emission dilution affects the real long-term yield. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real project, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying the Layer2 Sequencer: Who Packages Your Transaction, and Who Can Censor It
item.description: This article introduces research methods for Layer2/Rollup scaling: how to verify sequencer centralization risk, the difference between fraud-proof and validity-proof security models, whether withdrawal delay and forced-exit mechanisms actually work, and how to verify upgrade permissions and emergency mechanisms. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real project, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying NFT Floor Price: One Wash Trade Can Inflate the “Floor” by Multiples
item.description: This article introduces research methods for NFT collections: how to independently verify rarity rank and metadata, how to detect wash trading through wallet clustering, how to check the real liquidity depth behind a headline floor price, and how royalty enforcement differs across marketplaces. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not evaluate any real collection or marketplace, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying Lending Liquidation: How One Oracle Delay Triggers a Cascade
item.description: This article introduces research methods for lending-protocol liquidation mechanics: how health factor and liquidation thresholds are calculated, how to independently verify a protocol's real liquidation parameters, the economics behind liquidation bots, the price-feedback mechanics of cascading liquidations, and how to reconstruct a real liquidation event from on-chain data. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not draw conclusions about any real protocol or historical event, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Verifying DEX Liquidity: Beyond the TVL Number, What Is an AMM Pool's Real Depth
item.description: This article introduces research methods for DEX liquidity and on-chain swap prices, covering the division of labor between AMM pricing mechanisms and oracle price sources, how to independently verify the true depth of liquidity pools, how to calculate price impact and slippage, and the abstract mechanism categories behind MEV and sandwich attacks, along with a method for recording small-scale cross-chain test transactions and common misconceptions. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not draw conclusions about any real DEX, protocol, or historical event, does not constitute investment advice, and is not a guide for arbitrage or attack operations.

item.title: Verifying Airdrop Distribution: A High Address Count Can Still Be One Person's Sybil Farm
item.description: This article introduces research methods for airdrop and Sybil address detection: how to independently verify the true dispersion of a token distribution, identify Sybil signals such as address clustering and linked fund flows, survey the abstract categories of Sybil farming and evasion techniques, and cross-check a project's self-claimed anti-Sybil mechanisms against actual on-chain snapshot data — while clarifying several common misconceptions. This is purely a methodology explainer; it does not draw conclusions about any real airdrop campaign, project, or historical Sybil incident, does not constitute investment advice, and does not encourage or provide guidance on airdrop farming.

item.title: Cross-Chain Bridges: Assets Never Actually Cross — They're Locked on One Side, Minted on the Other
item.description: This article introduces a verification methodology for cross-chain bridge security research: how to locate a bridge's actual on-chain asset custody contracts, examine the validator set and multisig signer composition, cross-check the bridge's claimed total value locked against real on-chain balances, and map out the abstract categories of mechanisms behind past bridge exploits along with common misconceptions. The piece is strictly about research methodology, names no real bridge product or historical incident, and is not investment advice.

item.title: Verifying Oracle Prices: A Few Blocks of Feed Delay Is a Manipulator's Window
item.description: This article introduces research methods for oracles and price data, covering how to locate on-chain oracle contracts, read price-update frequency and staleness thresholds, cross-check multiple independent price sources, and understand the abstract mechanism categories behind price-manipulation risk along with common misconceptions. The entire piece is a methodology discussion only — it does not draw conclusions about any real protocol or historical event, and it is not investment advice.

item.title: Researching Team Background and Developer Activity: From Verifying Team Pages to Reading GitHub Signals
item.description: A systematic guide to researching a crypto project's team background and developer activity: how to cross-check identity claims disclosed on team pages, how to read GitHub commit history as a research signal, how to spot "activity theater" — signs of fabricated development activity — and what an anonymous team does and does not imply. This article is a methodology discussion only; it draws no conclusions about the credibility of any specific team or project and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: What Proof of Reserves Actually Proves: A Merkle Tree Verifies Inclusion, Not Solvency
item.description: A systematic walkthrough of proof-of-reserves research methodology for centralized exchanges: what Merkle tree mechanisms can and cannot prove, how to independently verify reserve wallet balances on a block explorer, and how to interpret the asset/liability ratio and custodial risk. This article is a methodology discussion only — it draws no conclusions about any specific exchange's solvency or security and is not investment advice.

item.title: DAO Governance: When Voting Power Concentrates in a Few Addresses, Does “Decentralized” Still Hold
item.description: A systematic walkthrough of DAO governance research methodology: how to read a governance proposal's lifecycle, understand voting power distribution and delegation, verify a DAO treasury's multisig and timelock configuration on-chain, and cross-check what a proposal claims against what actually executes on-chain. This is a methodology discussion only — it draws no conclusions about the governance quality or security of any specific DAO or project, and is not investment advice.

item.title: An Audit Report Says “Audited” — But Audited What, Exactly: From Conclusions to On-Chain Verification
item.description: A systematic walkthrough of how to research smart contract security and audit reports: how to critically read an audit report, identify risks in owner/admin permissions and upgradeable proxy patterns, verify multisig thresholds and timelock delays on-chain, and cross-check audit conclusions against a contract's actual on-chain state. This article is a methodology discussion only — it draws no security conclusions about any specific contract, project, or audit firm, and does not constitute investment advice.

item.title: Tokenomics Research: How Much Unlock Pressure Hides Behind the Circulating Supply Number
item.description: A systematic look at how to research tokenomics: reading supply figures, FDV, allocation structures, and vesting schedules, then cross-checking whitepaper claims against on-chain data. For research purposes only, not investment advice.

item.title: Paying for Crypto Research Tool Subscriptions: A Practical Guide to Virtual Credit Cards
item.description: A payment guide for crypto researchers on paying for on-chain data platforms and RPC node subscriptions with virtual credit cards funded by stablecoins, including hard criteria for choosing a provider and pitfalls to avoid. For research purposes only, not investment advice.

item.title: Crypto Project Research Methodology: Why a Single Source Is Never Enough
item.description: A reusable framework for researching crypto projects: clarify information sources, examine fundamentals, tokenomics, technology and ecosystem, and on-chain data, and check against a risk list to avoid common pitfalls. For research purposes only, not investment advice.

item.title: On-Chain Data Analysis: An Address Is Not an Identity, a Transaction Is Not Intent
item.description: A walkthrough of addresses, transactions, blocks, and contracts, covering how to read (and how to misread) core on-chain metrics like active addresses, transaction volume, TVL, holder concentration, exchange flows, and stablecoin supply. For research purposes only, not investment advice.

item.title: Stablecoins, Cross-Chain Bridges, and On-Chain Fund Flows: A Researcher's View of Asset Movement
item.description: Understand stablecoin types and how "same name, different chain" assets differ, how cross-chain bridges and swap aggregators work and where their risks lie, and how to read fund-flow signals like stablecoin issuance, bridge inflows/outflows, and whale addresses. For research purposes only, not investment advice.
